coredata.dc:title: Cationic carbon quantum dots derived from alginate for gene delivery: One-step synthesis and cellular uptake
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jie
Deng, Wenwen
Wang, Yan
Cao, Xia
Chen, Jingjing
Wang, Qiang
Xu, Wenqian
Du, Pan
Yu, Qingtong
Chen, Jiaxin
Spector, Myron
Yu, Jiangnan
Xu, Ximing
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), unlike semiconductor quantum dots, possess fine biocompatibility, excellent upconversion properties, high photostability and low toxicity. Here, we report multifunctional CQDs which were developed using alginate, 3% hydrogen peroxide and double distilled water through a facile, eco-friendly and inexpensive one-step hydrothermal carbonization route. In this reaction, the alginate served as both the carbon source and the cationization agent. The resulting CQDs exhibited strong and stable fluorescence with water-dispersible and positively-charged properties which could serve as an excellent DNA condensation. As non-viral gene vector being used for the first time, the CQDs showed considerably high transfection efficiency (comparable to Lipofectamine2000 and significantly higher than PEI, p <0.05) and negligible toxicity. The photoluminescence properties of CQDs also permitted easy tracking of the cellular-uptake. The findings showed that both caveolae- and clathrin-mediated endocytosis pathways were involved in the internalization process of CQDs/pDNA complexes. Taken together, the alginate-derived photoluminescent CQDs hold great potential in biomedical applications due to their dual role as efficient non-viral gene vectors and bioimaging probes.
Statement of Significance This manuscript describes a facile and simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization route for preparing optically tunable photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from a novel raw material, alginate. These CQDs enjoy low cytotoxicity, positive zeta potential, excellent ability to condense macromolecular DNA, and most importantly, notably high transfection efficiency. The interesting finding is that the negatively-charged alginate can convert into positively charged CQDs without adding any cationic reagents. The significance of this study is that the cationic carbon quantum dots play dual roles as both non-viral gene vectors and bioimaging probes at the same time, which are most desirable in many fields of applications such as gene therapy, drug delivery, and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gene delivery
Cellular uptake
Sodium alginate
Photoluminescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81503025 , 81273470 and 81473172 ), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China ( 20113227110012 ), Special Funds for 333 and 331 projects (BRA2013198), Industry-University-Research Institution Cooperation ( JHB2012-37 , GY2012049 , GY2013055 ) in Jiangsu Province and Zhenjiang City, Senior professionals foundation of Jiangsu University projects (13JDG007), Program for Scientific research innovation team in Colleges and Universities of Jiangsu Province and A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent properties of CQDs solutions. (a) Photographs of luminescent CQDs solutions under a 365nm UV lamp excitation; double distilled water was used as control. (b) UV–vis absorption (line A), excitation (line B), and photoluminescent emission (line C, excited at 320nm) spectra of the CQDs. (c) Excitation-dependent photoluminescent emission spectra of the CQDs recorded with different excitation wavelengths increasing from 300nm to 430nm, with10nm increments.
(a) Low and high-magnified (inset) TEM images of the CQDs; (b) a TEM image of CQDs/pDNA complexes (CQDs/pDNA weight ratio=40:1); (c-d) the diameter distribution of the CQDs and CQDs/pDNA complexes.

(a) XPS survey spectrum; (b) the high-resolution C1s peaks and fitting curves; (c) the UV absorption spectrum before and after β-elimination reaction of alginate; (d) the FT-IR spectra of the raw sodium alginate and the CQDs.

Zeta potential data. From left to right: free pDNA, two batches of alginate (alginate-1 and alginate-2), the corresponding two batches of CQDs (CQDs-1 and CQDs-2) and their complexes with pDNA (CQDs-1/pDNA and CQDs-2/pDNA). Values are the means±SD of three repeated experiments.

Agarose gel electrophoresis patterns of CQDs/pDNA complexes at different weight ratios, from left to right: DNA marker, free pDNA, CQDs/pDNA complexes with weight ratios of 1:1, 10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 100:1 and the pure CQDs, respectively.

Cytotoxicity of CQDs/pDNA complexes. Percentage of viable cells after treated with CQDs/pDNA complexes with weight ratios (10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 60:1 and 100:1, respectively). PEI (25KDa) and semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) with the same weight ratios, as well as the Lipofectamine2000 (performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions) were employed as controls. Values are the means±SD of three repeated experiments (*p <0.05, **p <0.01, Student’s t-test).

In vitro gene transfection efficiency of CQDs/pDNA complexes. The CQDs/pDNA complexes were prepared at the weight ratios of 10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 60:1 and 100:1, respectively. PEI (25KDa) and SQDs with the same weight ratios, as well as the Lipofectamine2000 (performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions) were employed as controls. Values are the means±SD of three repeated experiments (*p <0.05, Student’s t-test).

The effects of various inhibitors on the cellular uptake of CQDs/pDNA complexes. Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of cells treated with eight inhibitors (SOV, colchicine, DMA, NH4Cl, filipin III, glucose, CPZ, and genistein) before the transfection with CQDs/pDNA complexes (at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 514nm for blue, green and red fluorescence, respectively). Scale bars=20μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Process of entering cells. Laser scanning confocal microscopy images showing the process of CQDs/YOYO-1-pDNA complexes entering 3T6 cells and the nucleus. The images were captured 4, 6, and 8h after transfection (excitation wavelengths of 405 and 488nm for blue and green fluorescence, respectively). YOYO-1, which emits a green fluorescence, was used to label pDNA and identify its location in the nucleus. Scale bars=20μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Inhibitors and their function and concentration.

row.entry: Inhibits the Na+/H+ exchange required for macropinocytosis
Neutralizes endosomal pH and inhibits a low-pH dependent endosomal escape
Inhibit cellular functions by microtubule inhibition
Inhibits the interactions of cytoskeletal motors

head.ce:title: Cationic carbon quantum dots derived from alginate for gene delivery: One-step synthesis and cellular uptake

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, and Center for Drug/Gene Delivery and Tissue Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, and Center for Drug/Gene Delivery and Tissue Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Harvard Medical School, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, 75 Francis St, Boston, MA 02115, USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), unlike semiconductor quantum dots, possess fine biocompatibility, excellent upconversion properties, high photostability and low toxicity. Here, we report multifunctional CQDs which were developed using alginate, 3% hydrogen peroxide and double distilled water through a facile, eco-friendly and inexpensive one-step hydrothermal carbonization route. In this reaction, the alginate served as both the carbon source and the cationization agent. The resulting CQDs exhibited strong and stable fluorescence with water-dispersible and positively-charged properties which could serve as an excellent DNA condensation. As non-viral gene vector being used for the first time, the CQDs showed considerably high transfection efficiency (comparable to Lipofectamine2000 and significantly higher than PEI, p <0.05) and negligible toxicity. The photoluminescence properties of CQDs also permitted easy tracking of the cellular-uptake. The findings showed that both caveolae- and clathrin-mediated endocytosis pathways were involved in the internalization process of CQDs/pDNA complexes. Taken together, the alginate-derived photoluminescent CQDs hold great potential in biomedical applications due to their dual role as efficient non-viral gene vectors and bioimaging probes.

This manuscript describes a facile and simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization route for preparing optically tunable photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from a novel raw material, alginate. These CQDs enjoy low cytotoxicity, positive zeta potential, excellent ability to condense macromolecular DNA, and most importantly, notably high transfection efficiency. The interesting finding is that the negatively-charged alginate can convert into positively charged CQDs without adding any cationic reagents. The significance of this study is that the cationic carbon quantum dots play dual roles as both non-viral gene vectors and bioimaging probes at the same time, which are most desirable in many fields of applications such as gene therapy, drug delivery, and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, luminescent quantum dots (QDs) have triggered intensive interest owing to their great potential for biological application [1–3]. QDs have compelling advantages over conventional fluorescent dyes; for example, they possess excellent photostability, high photoluminescence quantum yield, wide range of excitation spectrum and long lifetime of fluorescence [4]. To date, the QDs have been most frequently used as fluorescent probes for bioimaging [5–9] but seldom been employed for drug/gene delivery. This discrepancy is most likely due to the fact that traditional QDs are produced from heavy-metal elements such as PbSe, CdS and CdSe, which suffers from poor biocompatibility and high toxicity [10–13]. On the other hand, most of the current non-viral gene vectors (such as calcium phosphate nanoparticles [14] and cationic polymers [15,16]) do not possess the property of self-imaging. Therefore, the fabrication of a new type of benign and visible emitter that allows co-operative drug/gene delivery and bioimaging [17] could be an interesting challenge.

Photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a newcomer from the carbon nonmaterials family, have been gathering considerable attention in the nanotechnology field due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, strong fluorescence, stable photoluminescence and broad excitation spectra [10,18]. CQDs may therefore be a promising alternative to the traditional toxic heavy metal-based semiconductor QDs in a wide range of applications. They can also be developed into positive surface charges through appropriate modifications, which can act as a self-tracking drug/gene carrier.

To date, a considerable number of carbon source materials, including glycerol [19], polyvinyl pyrrolidone [12], candle soot [20], carbon fibers [21], citric acid [22], and various carbohydrate-based food caramels (e.g., bread, jaggery, sugar caramel, corn flakes, and biscuits) have been employed to construct CQDs [23]. Additionally, various approaches to synthesizing CQD have also been developed, such as the top-down methods of laser ablation [24], high-energy ion beam irradiation [25], and electrochemical oxidation [26] and the bottom-up methods of microwave-assisted synthesis [27–29], hydrothermal synthesis [30], and combustion thermal oxidation [20]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal synthetic route has been widely applied because of its simple procedure, low cost and green properties [31,32].

Herein, we report on CQDs prepared by a one-step, green and low-cost process by relying on the hydrothermal carbonization of a new CQDs carbon source, namely, sodium alginate. The CQDs were designed to serve two purposes: as a gene vector to deliver plasmid TGF-β1 into 3T6 cells and as a fluorescent probe to directly monitor the gene delivery process so as to shed light on the cellular uptake mechanisms of the CQDs/pDNA complexes. Sodium alginate, a natural polysaccharide extracted from seaweed, has been widely used in food, medicine, textile, printing and dyeing, paper making, and daily chemical products [33,34]. Recently, it has been employed as an important pharmaceutic adjuvant and biomedical material [35–37]. Previous studies used alginate to prepare gene/drug delivery formulations to achieve selective cell targeting [38,39]. However, it has never been used as a carbon source to produce CQDs. The alginate could also serve as a prototype for the development of natural polysaccharide into multi-functional materials of bioimaging and targeted delivery of gene/drugs. Therefore, it is prudent to explore the potential of alginate as a desirable material for producing CQDs with dual functions (gene delivery and bioimaging).

Based on the self-tracking ability, the cellular uptake pathways of CQDs have been investigated. Previous reports have revealed several mechanisms associated with cellular uptake of nanoparticles, including clathrin/caveolae-mediated endocytosis, clathrin/caveolae-independent endocytosis, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, cell surface ruffling-mediated endocytosis and specialized endocytosis via cell membrane receptors [40–42]. Generally, nanoparticles may be taken up by cells via size and shape selectivity, surface coating and charge, and cell lines [43]. Upon entering the cells, nanoparticles are transported via typical cellular processes from early endosomes to late endosomes or lysosomes to endosomal escape and dissociation of vector/gene complexes, which is often considered a rate-limiting step for the efficient transport of genes to the nucleus [44–46]. The CQDs prepared in the present study is expected to serve as an efficient gene carrier. Therefore, it is crucial to explore the pathways responsible for their cellular uptake.

The CQDs were also fully characterized in terms of morphology, size, zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, fluorescence emission spectra and absorbance spectra. Enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay (ELISA) and confocal laser scanning microscopy were further used to evaluate gene transfection and effects of cellular uptake inhibition, respectively. Compared with previous reports in which QDs were mostly investigated using their luminescent properties and applications in bioimaging [9,10,18], sodium alginate-derived CQDs in this study not only show low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, but also act as both an effective gene vector and a fluorescent probe without additional modifications. Altogether, the findings will provide a new source for CQDs generation and solid evidence for the application of CQDs as an effective non-viral gene vector and bioimaging probe.

Two different batches of sodium alginate (CAS number: 9005-38-3, analytical reagent and biological reagent) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (3%), glycerin (ultrapure, molecular biology grade), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), quinine, sulfuric acid solution (H2SO4), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), sodium orthovanadate (SOV), glucose, and colchicine were purchased from Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. of China National Pharmaceutical Group (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), trypsin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco BRL (Invitrogen Co., Carlsbad, CA, USA). The TGF-β1 ELISA kit was provided by Yantai Science and Biotechnology Co., Ltd., (Shandong, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma (Sigma, USA). Chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ), 5-(N,N-dimethyl)-amiloride (DMA), and genistein were obtained from ChromaDex (ChromaDex, USA). YOYO-1 was purchased from Invitrogen (Invitrogen, USA). Semiconductor quantum dots (cadmium telluride quantum dots, CdTe QDs), were kindly provided by Doctor Shanshan Tong at School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. The structure of the CdTe QDs is as shown in Fig. S1.

All animal experimental protocols were in accordance with the Helsinki Convention, which were duly approved by the Jiangsu University Ethics Committee for the use of experimental animals and conformed to the Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals.

Sodium alginate, as a carbon source, together with DDW and hydrogen peroxide, was employed to produce CQDs via a simple hydrothermal carbonization route. The optimal formulation and reaction conditions were obtained based on preliminary experiments (data not shown). Specifically, 0.25g of sodium alginate and 2.5mL of 3% hydrogen peroxide were mixed together in double distilled water (10mL) in a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner with a 100-mL capacity, followed by ultrasonication to form a homogeneous solution. The autoclave was sealed and heated to 220°C for 12h. The reactor was then allowed to cool at room temperature, and the resulting solution diluted with double distilled water (DDW, 10mL) and dialyzed (MW cut-off 1000Da, Shanghai Green Bird Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) against frequently replenished (every 2h) DDW for 48h. After dialysis, the sample was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter followed, by freeze-drying which led to the production of CQDs with excellent fluorescence performance.

The CQD powder was dissolved in DDW to form a 2mg/mL solution and filtered through a 0.22-μm filter to become sterilized. Plasmid TGF-β1 (pTGF-β1), as a model gene, was diluted with sterilized water to obtain the working solution (400μg/mL). The CQDs/pTGF-β1 complexes at various weight ratios (CQDs to pTGF-β1) were formulated by adding equal volume of CQDs solution (100μL, diluted with sterilized water to obtain the appropriate concentrations) to the pTGF-β1 solution (100μL) which was vortex mixed for 1min. The mixtures were then kept at room temperature for 30min. Then, the CQDs/pTGF-β1 complexes were obtained and stored at 4°C for subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence of the CQDs was observed using ZF-2 Ultraviolet Analyzer (Shanghai Anting Electronic Instrument Factory, China). The fluorescence emission spectra were examined with a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU, Japan). The absorbance spectra were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, SHIMADZU, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on an AVATAR 370 (Nicolet, USA) using KBr pellets as the sample matrix in the frequency range of 500–4000cm−1. The elemental analyses were conducted via Euro EA elemental analyzer (Eurovector, Milan, Italy). The particle size and zeta potential were measured using a ZetaPLus Brookhaven Instruments (Brookhaven Instruments Corp., Holtsville, NY, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was performed via Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer using 150W Al K Alpha as the X-ray source (1486.6eV), with the energy step size of 0.100eV. Deconvolution of the XPS spectra was achieved by fitting the data with a Gaussian/Lorentzian combination of peak shapes with variation in full peak width at half the maximum value. The peak identification was determined with reference to an XPS data base or from compounds analyzed in the same apparatus.

HR-TEM images of the CQDs and CQDs/pTGF-β1 complexes were captured using a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 40–100kV. Briefly, samples were prepared by placing 1μL of suspension onto a copper screen and subsequently air drying the suspension. The dried samples were observed directly under the TEM.

The quantum yield of the CQDs was determined by comparing the particles’ integrated photoluminescence intensities as previously reported [10]. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield: 54%) was selected as a standard. Samples were dissolved in ultrapure water at different concentrations. The integrated photoluminescence intensity is the area under the photoluminescence (PL) curve over the excitation wavelength range. The quantum yields were calculated according to the following equation:
Yu = Ys ∗ ( Fu / Fs ) ∗ ( As / Au )
where the subscripts “s” and “u” refer to quinine sulfate and CQDs, respectively; “Y” is the fluorescence quantum yield; “F” represents the integrated photoluminescence intensity; and A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 488nm.

CQDs/pTGF-β1 complex solutions with their respective weight ratios (1:1, 10:1, 20:1, 40:1 and 100:1) were prepared as described in Section 2.3. The samples (10mL each) were mixed with 2μL of loading buffer (0.1% sodium dodecyl sulfate, 5% glycerol, and 0.005% bromophenol blue) and applied to a 1% agarose gel in a Trisborate-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid buffer solution (TBE, pH 8.0) containing 0.1mg/mL ethidium bromide (EtBr) at 80V for 40min. The gel was imaged with a UV transilluminator (Gel Doc 2000, BIO RAD laboratories, Hercules, CA) at a wavelength of 365nm.

The 3T6 cells were purchased from the Cell Bank at the Chinese Academy of Science (Shanghai, China) and maintained in DMEM/10% FBS medium (containing 10% FBS, 100U/mL penicillin-streptomycin) at 37°C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere for experiments.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the CQDs/pTGF-β1 complexes was assessed using an MTT assay in the 3T6 cells. Specifically, 3T6 cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates (Corning, USA) at a density of 2×104 cells/well and incubated for 24h at 37°C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. Afterwards, the culture medium in each well was replaced with fresh DMEM/10%FBS (90μL), and CQDs/pTGF-β1 complex solutions (10μL, containing 0.2μg pTGF-β1 per well) with various weight ratios (CQDs/pTGF-β1=10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 60:1 and 80:1) were added to each well, followed by incubation for 72h. PEI (25KDa), Lipofectamine2000 and the semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) were employed as positive control groups. An aliquot (20μL) of MTT solution (5mg/mL) was added to each well. After 4-h incubation, the medium was removed and 100μL of dimethyl sulfoxide added to each well to form a formazan crystal salt solution. The absorbance of each well was measured at 570nm using an Epoch Microplate Reader (BioTek, USA).

The 3T6 cells were seeded as Section 2.9. When conducting transfection experiments, the medium in each well was replaced with serum-free medium (100μL) for at least 4h. CQDs/pTGF-β1 complexes at various weight ratios (10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 60:1 and 80:1) were then added to each well (containing 0.2μg pTGF-β1 per well) respectively, and commercially available transfection reagents, PEI (25KDa) and Lipofectamine2000, as well as the SQDs, were used as positive controls. After incubation at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 4h, the medium was replaced with DMEM/10%FBS (100μL for each well). The transfected cells were re-incubated for 68h. The supernatant in each well was separately collected in a 500-μL Eppendorf tube and centrifuged for 5min at 1500rpm. The supernatant was transferred to a new Eppendorf tube. ELISA assay was conducted according to the procedures provided by the manufacturer. The data were collected using a microplate reader (SpectraMax 190, MD, Utah, USA).

Based on previous reports, there are five main types of pathways responsible for cellular uptake of nano-materials, namely, clathrin-dependent endocytosis, caveolae (or caveolae/raft)-mediated endocytosis, Na+/H+ exchange-dependent macropinocytosis, microtubule-dependent active transport and cytoskeletal motors-mediated transport [47,48]. To thoroughly investigate the intracellular pathways, eight inhibitors were used to examine the cellular uptake pathways of the CQDs, with chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ) and glucose for inhibiting the clathrin-dependent endocytosis [49], filipin III and genistein for interfering the caveolae (or caveolae/raft)-mediated endocytosis [50], NH4Cl for disturbing the transport of acidic endosomes and lysosomes [51], colchicine for inhibiting the microtubule-dependent active transport [52], SOV for interrupting the cytoskeletal motors-mediated transport [53], and 5-(N,N-dimethyl)-amiloride (DMA) for disturbing the Na+/H+ exchange-dependent macropinocytosis [54]. Three different concentrations of each inhibitor were used. The detailed information about the optimization of inhibitor concentrations is presented as Supplementary Information.

Each solution of the inhibitor was diluted by serum-free DMEM medium. The 3T6 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate at a density of 1×105 cells/well as mentioned in Section 2.9. When the cells reach a 80–90% confluence, the medium in each well was replaced with the corresponding inhibitor solution (500μL per well). Thirty (30) minutes later, 20μL of CQDs/pDNA complexes solution (weight ratio 40:1, and 0.8μg of pDNA per well) was added to each well and incubated at 37°C for 2h. The culture medium in each well was then replaced with 10% serum-containing DMEM medium. After incubation for 24h, the cells were observed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS-NT, Leica, Heidelberg, Germany) at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 514nm, respectively. Control tests were carried out by adding the mixture of CQDs, pDNA and YOYO-1 using the same procedure.

The 3T6 cells were seeded at an initial density of 1×105 cells/well in 24-well plates and incubated at 37°C for 24h in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. YOYO-1 was mixed with free DNA before preparing CQDs/pDNA complexes at various weight ratios. The resulting samples were applied to each well. At different time points (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 12 h) of incubation at 37°C, the cells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) and fixed with a 4% paraformaldehyde solution for 0.5h. Finally, nucleus transshipment of the CQDs/pDNA was visualized under a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS-NT, Leica, Heidelberg, Germany) at excitation wavelengths of 405 and 488nm for the blue and green fluorescence, respectively.

All data were reported as mean±standard deviation (SD). The Students t-test was conducted to determine the significance (a p <0.05 was considered statistically significant) of the differences between the selected groups using the statistical software of SPSS 14.0 (SPSS company, USA).

In the current study, the prepared CQDs gave an average fluorescence quantum yield of 12.7%. The water-soluble CQDs emitted obvious blue fluorescence when illuminated with an ultraviolet lamp at an excitation wavelength of 365nm (Fig.1 a). The results also revealed a broad emission peak centered at 450nm with an excitation wavelength of 297nm (Fig.1b). The maximum emission peaks of the CQDs shifted from 447nm to 530nm with different excitation wavelengths (increasing from 300nm to 430nm), Fig.1c. The results indicated that the photoluminescent emission was dependent on the excitation wavelength, with a red shift being observed when using a longer wavelength of excitation.

The HR-TEM images demonstrated that the CQDs were monodispersed and spherical in morphology (Fig.2 a, insert). The CQDs also had centralized size distribution within the range of 5–10nm (Fig.2a). Similarly, the CQDs/pDNA complexes showed a spherical shape and an average particle size smaller than 50nm (Fig.2b). Obviously, the size of CQDs/pDNA complexes increased compared with the CQDs, which indicated that there was slight aggregation of CQDs during the combination process with the plasmid DNA. Based on the DLS analysis, the average diameters of CQDs and CQDs/pDNA complexes gave 7.3±1.4nm and 20.5±3.1nm, respectively (Fig.2c and d), which were consistent with the TEM. These findings indicated that the CQDs have a strong condensation effect on macromolecular pDNA which could benefit the process of cellular uptake.

The predominant peaks at 286.0, 399.1 and 531.1eV of the XPS survey spectrum were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, which indicated the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements (Fig.3 a). The high resolution of the C1s spectrum exhibited the peaks at 248.6, 286.1and 288.7eV, which also implied that the CQDs were functionalized with CC, COH and CO groups (Fig.3b). These findings revealed that the material source (sodium alginate) has an N-containing impurity. The elemental analysis (C, H, N) demonstrated that the C (29.52%), H (4.88%) and N (0.02%) were for sodium alginate batch 1 (analytical reagent), and C (30.38%), H (4.69%) and N (0.02%) for batch 2 (biological reagent), which further confirmed the existence of the N-containing impurity. Presumably, this nitrogen impurity could be a glycoprotein which is a common impurity in natural polysaccharides. To verify this hypothesis, β-elimination reaction was performed by treating the sodium alginate with NaOH solution (0.2M). It is well known that glycopeptide linkages connect the carbohydrate and protein chains in the glycoproteins. There are two types of glycopeptide linkages, namely N-glycopeptide linkages (insensitive to alkaline solution) and O-glycopeptide linkages (sensitive to alkaline solution). The glycoproteins with O-glycopeptide linkages would be hydrolyzed at the site of serine or threonine when treated with alkaline solution. This situation results in a β-elimination reaction which refers to the enhanced ultraviolet absorption at the wavelength of 240nm because of the formation of unsaturated amino acids during the reaction [55]. In this work, the β-elimination reaction showed that there was an obvious enhancement of the peak around 240nm in the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the sodium alginate after treatment with NaOH solution (0.2M) compared with the untreated sample (Fig.3c). This finding confirmed that the impurity was glycoprotein with the existence of O-glycopeptide linkages.

To obtain structural insights into the CQDs, the FT-IR spectra of the CQDs and sodium alginate were characterized. The bands at 3390cm−1 (caused by OH vibration), 1625cm−1 (attributed to CO) and 1100cm−1 (the existence of CO) demonstrated that the CQDs inherited most of the functional groups from the sodium alginate (original material), Fig.3d.

Based on the zeta potential analysis, the CQDs prepared from the two different batches of sodium alginate displayed positive charges, whereas the untreated material produced negative values (Fig. 4 ). The positive charges were probably due to the presence of glycoproteins. It is a common knowledge that positive surface charges are beneficial for DNA condensation [56,57]. As expected, the CQDs showed an excellent ability to condense the macromolecular pDNA into ultra small nanoparticles (<50nm) as described above. The CQDs/pDNA complexes also exhibited positive charges which indicated that a charge reversal occurred in the negatively-charged pDNA when combined with the positively-charged CQDs. With the presence of glycoprotein impurity, the sodium alginate could serve as both the carbon source and the cationization agent, which is highly desirable for the development of CQDs-based gene vectors.

Agarose gel electrophoresis was carried out to demonstrate the ability of the CQDs to bind with pDNA. The pDNA migration gradually decreased with increase in weight ratios (CQDs/pDNA), and when the ratio was equal to or greater than 40:1 (well 6 and 7), there was almost no sign of pDNA migration (Fig. 5 ). However, most of the free pDNA (well 1) migrated across the gel which yielded a bright band that indicated the location of the pDNA. Additionally, the pure CQDs showed a reversed electrophoretic mobility (well 8), which verified the positive surface charge on the CQDs. In summary, the CQDs were able to effectively combine with the pDNA at a low weight ratio (40:1), which is a crucial property for an effective gene vector.

However, almost completely diminished fluorescence intensity was observed in cells treated with the other 6 inhibitors (CPZ, filipin III, genistein, glucose, NH4Cl), which suggested that the cellular uptake of the CQDs/pDNA complexes resulted from the combined effects of multiple pathways. Filipin III can block caveolae/raft-mediated endocytosis by interacting with 3-β-hydroxysterols in the plasma membrane to form filipin-sterol complexes and subsequently cause the filamentous caveolin-1-coat to rapidly disassemble, resulting in the inhibition of caveolae-mediated endocytosis [50]. The cellular uptake of the CQDs/pDNA complexes was also disrupted by the presence of filipin III which implied that caveolae-mediated endocytosis may be responsible for this event. Genistein, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, can locally disrupt the actin network at the endocytic site and inhibit the recruitment of dynamin II, which is known to be indispensable for caveolae-mediated uptake [61,62]. The significantly reduced fluorescence intensity that resulted from genistein further confirmed that caveolae-mediated endocytosis contributed to the uptake of the CQDs/pDNA complexes. CPZ is known for its inhibition of clathrin-mediated endocytosis by reversibly translocating clathrin and its adapter proteins from the plasma membrane to intracellular vesicles [49]. Glucose inhibits clathrin-dependent endocytosis [63]. The inhibition resulting from CPZ and glucose suggested that a clathrin-mediated endocytosis pathway was involved in the internalization of the CQDs/pDNA complexes. Moreover, NH4Cl was employed due to its ability to neutralize the pH value of endosomal and lysosomal within a very short period to inhibit a low-pH-dependent endosomal escape [51]. The impairment of cellular uptake caused by NH4Cl indicated the involvement of endolysosomal trafficking in the internalization of the CQDs/pDNA complexes, which represented a classic consequence of clathrin-mediated endocytosis [64]. This result confirmed the contribution of clathrin-mediated endocytosis to the uptake of the CQDs/pDNA complexes. It is well recognized that multiple factors, such as particle size and shape, surface charge, and the type of particles and cells, can significantly contribute to the internalization of particles. In this study, the cellular uptake process of the CQDs/pDNA complexes involved a combination of caveolae- and clathrin-mediated endocytosis. The multiple pathways may greatly contribute to the CQDs internalization, but exact mechanism of intracellular trafficking remains elusive, which requires further research in future.

Confocal microscopy was conducted to investigate the localization of exogenous DNA. YOYO-1 was used as a fluorescent probe (green fluorescence) to indicate the position of exogenous DNA inside the nucleus because CQDs cannot enter it. Fig. 9 showed the images captured by a laser scanning confocal microscope with the excitation wavelengths at 405 and 488nm for various time points (4, 6 and 8h) after transfection with CQDs/pDNA-YOYO-1 complexes. Specifically, 4h after transfection, some of the CQDs/pDNA-YOYO-1 complexes entered the cells and a few were located within the nucleus (green fluorescence). As time passed, more and more CQDs were located at the periphery of the nucleus as shown by the blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, significantly increased green dots appeared in the nucleus, which suggested that an elevated amount of pDNA (labeled with YOYO-1) was released from CQDs/pDNA-YOYO-1 complexes into the nucleus where the pDNA can achieve effective and stable expression. This data demonstrated that the CQDs/pDNA complexes could achieve high transfection efficiency via timely release of pDNA from the CQDs/pDNA complexes in the periphery of the nucleus, with the pDNA subsequently entering the nucleus for effective expression of the target proteins, whereas the CQDs were kept outside the nucleus.

In summary, we developed optically tunable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by the combination of sodium alginate, double distilled water and 3% hydrogen peroxide through a facile, inexpensive and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal carbonization route. Owing to the excellent photoluminescent properties and positive surface charge, the CQDs developed in the present study could play dual roles as both bioimaging probes and gene vectors at the same time. The results revealed that the CQDs had a strong capacity to condense plasmid DNA, fine biocompatibility, low toxicity and high transfection efficiency. These photoluminescent CQDs could also track their own paths of entering into the cells. The cellular uptake evaluation revealed that both caveolae- and clathrin-mediated endocytosis pathways were involved in the cellular uptake of CQDs/pDNA complexes. Altogether, the cationic CQDs prepared in this work hold a great promise for gene delivery and bioimaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81503025, 81273470 and 81473172), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (20113227110012), Special Funds for 333 and 331 projects (BRA2013198), Industry-University-Research Institution Cooperation (JHB2012-37, GY2012049, GY2013055) in Jiangsu Province and Zhenjiang City, Senior professionals foundation of Jiangsu University projects (13JDG007), Program for Scientific research innovation team in Colleges and Universities of Jiangsu Province and A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2016.06.021.
